title: St. Onuphrius (Abba Nofer) the Great — St. Peter of Athos & St. Antonina the Martyr
primary_saint: St. Onuphrius the Great of Egypt the Desert Hermit, St. Peter of Athos the Solitary, and St. Antonina of Nicaea the Virgin-Martyr
narrative:
On the twelfth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy and venerable Father Onuphrius the Great, known in the Ethiopian tradition as Abba Nofer, one of the most celebrated of all the hermits of the Egyptian desert and the supreme representative in the tradition of the anachoretic life pressed to its absolute limit. Onuphrius, according to the account of the monk Paphnutius who found him in the deep desert and preserved his testimony before his death, had lived alone in the desert for sixty or seventy years without contact with any other human being, subsisting on dates from a palm tree beside his cell and water from a spring, his body so wasted by fasting and his beard and hair so grown that they covered him like a garment. Paphnutius found him by following a distant light and saw him approaching from afar as something between a human being and an apparition — a figure so reduced by the life of pure prayer that the body had become almost transparent to the spirit it housed. Onuphrius told Paphnutius that he had gone to the desert as a young monk from the cenobitic community of Hermopolis, that an angel had guided him to the place where he lived, and that for all the years of his solitude the Eucharist had been brought to him by the angels on Saturdays and Sundays.

The theology of Onuphrius in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the radical simplification: the hermit who has stripped away every human convenience and social bond has not impoverished himself but enriched himself beyond measure, because he has made room in himself for the one thing that is needful. The tradition's account of Onuphrius is at pains to specify that his solitude was not a rejection of the Church or the sacraments but a different mode of participation in them: the Eucharist came to him from the angels when he could not go to it from the community, and the Church that he left behind in the cenobium was the same Church that sustained him in the deep desert, present to him in ways that transcended the ordinary channels of presence. Onuphrius is the tradition's most extreme statement that the Church is not contained by its visible structures, that the grace of God reaches to the uttermost boundaries of the inhabited and uninhabited world, and that the man who has gone farthest from human society has not gone farthest from God.

Saint Peter of Athos is the tradition's great pioneer of the Athonite monastic life: the monk who, in the ninth century, was the first to establish a permanent hermitage on the Holy Mountain of Athos, the peninsula in northern Greece that would become the most concentrated site of Orthodox monasticism in the world. Peter was a soldier who was captured in battle and imprisoned, and who vowed during his imprisonment to embrace the monastic life if he were freed. His liberation was attributed to the intercession of St. Nicholas and the Theotokos, and he fulfilled his vow by travelling to Rome, receiving the monastic habit from the pope, and then — guided by a vision of the Theotokos herself — making his way to the peninsula of Athos, where he lived as a hermit for fifty or more years. Peter of Athos is the tradition's founding solitary of what became a community: the individual hermit whose witness drew others to the same mountain and whose solitude was, paradoxically, the seed of the great cenobitic and idiorrhythmic communities that would make Athos the spiritual capital of Eastern Orthodoxy.

Saint Antonina of Nicaea is a virgin-martyr of the Great Persecution whose brief but vivid account in the tradition includes a remarkable episode of fraternal martyrdom: condemned to be placed in a sack and thrown into a lake, Antonina was rescued at the last moment by a Christian soldier named Alexander who substituted himself for her — putting her in his own soldier's clothing and placing himself in the sack in her place. The exchange was discovered, and both Alexander and Antonina died together: she by drowning and he by fire. Antonina's story is the tradition's most dramatic instance of the martyr who inspires another martyr in the very moment of execution, whose willingness to die moved a soldier to take her place and thereby claim for himself a death more glorious than any battlefield could have provided. The twelfth day of Sene thus gathers four modes of the solitary and self-giving life: the great hermit of the Egyptian deep desert, the pioneer solitary of the Holy Mountain, and the virgin and soldier who died together in a city on the sea. Amen.
